title: The Day Leo Stopped Thinking
description: Leo's AI homework helper is incredible — until the day it isn't there, and Leo realises he can't remember how to think without it.

steps.content:
Leo is ten years old and brilliant at most things — except sitting still long enough to think. When his school started letting students use an AI homework helper, Leo was thrilled. Within a week, he had a routine: type the question, read the answer, copy it down. His grades went up. His teachers were impressed. Leo felt like a genius.

But three months later, his teacher Mrs. Okafor put a single question on the board for a timed test — no devices allowed. Leo stared at it. It was the kind of question he'd answered twenty times with the AI. Alone, without it, his mind was blank.

steps.question: What had Leo actually been practising every day for three months?

steps.content:
After the test, Leo talked to Mrs. Okafor. She wasn't angry. She asked him a question: 'When you used the AI, did you ever ask yourself why the answer was right — or did you just check that it matched what you expected?'

Leo thought hard. He'd never done that. He'd used the AI the same way some people use a calculator they don't understand — as a magic box. He got the output. He never understood the process.

'AI is a brilliant thinking partner,' Mrs. Okafor said, 'but only if you're doing the thinking too.'

steps.instruction: Leo wants to use AI properly from now on. Which of these is the RIGHT way to use an AI homework helper?
options: Ask it for the answer, copy it, move on
Ask it to explain the method step by step, then try the next similar problem yourself
Use it only when the teacher isn't watching
Ask it to write the whole essay and change a few words

steps.content:
Leo changed his approach. Instead of asking the AI for answers, he started asking it to explain things differently when he was stuck — then closed it and tried the problem himself. If he got it wrong, he went back and asked where his thinking broke down.

His grades dipped a little at first. But six weeks later, he sat another no-device test. He didn't get every question right. But he wasn't blank. He had thoughts. He had a process. He had his own mind back — and the AI had helped him build it, not replace it.

The best tools make you stronger. The worst ones make you forget you were ever strong.
